Are You Smarter Than Claude? · Index

A Working Session

Are You
Smarter Than
Claude?

How an AI thinks, stores, and uses context —
and how to write docs that work for the model, not against it.

The Analogy · One Road Trip

It's Just A Car

PART

—

—

Beat 0 / 9 Model:
Your Job All Session

Grab A
Sticky Note

For the rest of this session, you are the harness. Every time I ask the room something, run it through the three questions and jot what survives on your note.

Fast Weights ▸ Me

  • weather
  • tomorrow
  • location?
  • units (°F)
Three Questions · Your Guide All Session

1 · The
Frequency Filter

"How often have I seen this word?"

This isolates the incoming information density. By asking it, you actively filter out structural background noise to spot the rare, high-value data points worth keeping.

On your sticky notes: when a word keeps recurring, it's a keeper — write it down. The one-offs are usually noise.
Three Questions · 2 of 3

2 · The
Boundary Trigger

"Does this set of words change the subject?"

This establishes your context window. It's your tripwire — signaling exactly when to flush outdated information and prep your working memory for a new trajectory.

On your sticky notes: when the subject shifts, draw a line. That boundary tells you what context no longer belongs.
Three Questions · 3 of 3

3 · The
Resonance Link

"Does my human brain say this word relates to the current subject?"

This executes the contextual join. It forces a direct evaluation between new data and your active working memory — deciding whether the new info should amplify the current state or be kept separate.

On your sticky notes: draw a pointer between words that belong together. Those links are the relationships the model needs spelled out.
How The Harness Remembers

Fast Weights
vs Slow Weights

The model is the engine. The harness is everything wrapped around it, deciding what to load and what to drop. It pulls from two kinds of memory, and both have technical names.

Fast Weights · Short-Term

The Sticky Notes

The live conversation — what's visible right now. Temporary, rapidly-changing parameters that wire up on the fly, then fade when you close the tab.

Fast, fluid, disposable. Your working scratchpad.

Slow Weights · Long-Term

The Handout Docs

Memories, documents, knowledge bases — and the deep parameters learned through training. Persistent knowledge the model carries everywhere, changed only by retraining.

Durable, authoritative. Survives the session.

The move that matters: deliberately promote what's worth keeping from your sticky notes (fast) into your long-term docs (slow). fast ➜ slow is how context survives past the moment it was useful.
Watch Words Fire Together

Same Room,
One Pattern

Do the neurons — the words — fire together? Strong overlap keeps repeating across many prompts. Weak overlap fires once and drifts.

Prompt 1What's the difference between latte and cappuccino?
Prompt 2Is the weather going to be humid this week?
Prompt 3What's a good substitute for buttermilk in this recipe?
Prompt 4Give me tomorrow's forecast in celsius.
Prompt 5How long should I marinate the chicken?
Prompt 6Should I bring an umbrella tonight?
Prompt 7What's the forecast look like for the weekend?
Prompt 8Can you suggest a podcast about ancient rome?
Prompt 9What's the weather tomorrow?
Prompt 10How's the forecast looking for the commute?
Prompt 11Will there be frost on the windshield tomorrow?
Prompt 12Will the weather clear up by noon tomorrow?
Prompt 13What's the score in the game right now?
Prompt 14Is my flight still on time?
Prompt 15What time does the dentist appointment start?
Prompt 16Is a storm rolling in?
Prompt 17How do I reset my email password?
Prompt 18Can you proofread this paragraph for typos?
Prompt 19How windy will it be tomorrow morning?
Prompt 20Is there a chance of hail later?
Prompt 21What's a quick stretch routine before the gym?
Prompt 22Can you summarize this pdf into three bullet points?
Prompt 23Will it snow tonight, or just flurries?
Prompt 24Is the weather sunny or cloudy this weekend?
Prompt 25What's tomorrow looking like for the coast?
Prompt 26What temperature will it be this afternoon?
Prompt 27Give me the forecast for tomorrow, will it rain?
Prompt 28Can you check if this invoice total is correct?
Prompt 29What's the weather doing this evening?
Prompt 30Give me a forecast by the hour.
Prompt 31What's a good book on behavioral economics?
Prompt 32How do I revert a git commit without losing my changes?
weak strong color & length = how often the word fired

weather · tomorrow · forecast only turn up in about a fifth of these 32 prompts — plenty of others aren't even about weather — but they're still the most repeated → wired in. Everything else fires once or twice → eviction candidates.

Where Hebb Falls Short · The Road To Mamba

Building A
Better Memory

“Neurons that fire together, wire together.” — Donald Hebb, 1949

Hebb tells you words relate, not in what order or which caused the next. So memory got built up, one fix at a time — picture the same note-taking tool getting better at holding a long story. Each step solves the last one's problem. The final step is Mamba.

One Sticky Note

Holds a single thought. Write a new one and the old note falls off.

✗ Can't remember the start of the story.

→so add pages

A Notepad

Now it has pages, so thoughts last longer. But you flip back and forth to find anything.

✗ Slow, and long stories still don't fit.

→so spread it all out

Every Page On The Wall

All pages out at once — you see everything, nothing's lost.

✗ The wall keeps growing — slow & costly.

→so summarize

One Tidy Summary

Keeps a single running summary of the whole story — all the memory, none of the sprawl.

✓ Long memory that stays fast & cheap.

The pattern goes more memory, then more reach, then lower cost. Mamba is where it finally balances out — Transformer-level memory, but without re-reading the whole conversation every single time. It's the architecture behind the newest long-context models.

In ML terms: sticky note = RNN (Recurrent Neural Network) · notepad = LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) / GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) · every page on the wall = Transformer · tidy summary = Mamba (a Selective State-Space Model)

Mamba · The Fourth Thing

Holding
The Thread

Your three questions size up each word on its own. But Mamba's real trick is the fourth thing — it keeps a running summary of everything so far, instead of re-reading the whole conversation each time.

Question 1

Frequency

"How often have I seen this word?"

Question 2

Boundary

"Does this change the subject?"

Question 3

Resonance

"Does this relate to the current subject?"

The Fourth · NEW

The Thread

"What came before, and how does it carry forward?"

The first three judge each word in isolation. The fourth holds the story so far — the order things happened and how each step led to the next. That's the "how they relate" your posted answer needed:

prompt eng1 · how you ask
→
harness2 · loads context
→
context3 · what model sees
→
answer4 · resolves
A pile of related words can't tell you "how do they relate." A running thread can, because it remembers that this led to that, which led to the answer. That thread is exactly what the Smart Assistant holds onto and the Group Chat has to reconstruct from scratch every time.
The Context Window · Live

What is IBNR? (The 16-Spot Parking Lot)

The context window holds 16 tokens, like 16 parking spots. Six real questions go in, one batch at a time — watch which words get kept and which get towed away.

Press “Process Batch 1” to send the first question →
Hold on — are we changing the subject? A hotdog has nothing to do with IBNR. I'll note it, but it's probably not worth keeping.
NEW OLD RARE ★ VIP RESERVED
Batch Parked: 0/16 Fell out: 0 Top: —

Why it works this way

  • Finite space. 16 spots — the model literally cannot hold everything.
  • Frequency. A word that keeps re-appearing gets reinforced and defends its spot.
  • Recency. Stale one-offs age NEW → OLD, then get evicted to free space — the same idea behind the /compact command, which clears out aging context to make room.
  • Rarity. A few distinctive words (up to four) earn a RARE badge and resist eviction — distinctiveness carries weight, even at low frequency.
  • VIP reserved parking. When a rare word also recurs often, it gets promoted into a reserved spot up top — held no matter how full the lot gets. The empty reserved spots wait until a word earns one.

▸ Process a batch to reveal the next rule.

Context Rot

Context Rot

Context rot is the drop in output quality that happens as input gets longer, even when the window isn't close to full.

Length alone causes it

A 2025 study from Chroma tested 18 frontier models across 8 input lengths. Accuracy fell at every length, not just near the limit. A 1-million-token window degrades by 50,000 tokens in. A bigger window gives a model more room to fill with noise. It doesn't stop the rot.

More tokens in doesn't mean better answers out.

Position changes accuracy too

Stanford researchers measured accuracy by where the answer sits in the context window. The curve is U-shaped, and six model families show the same pattern:

Accuracy is highest at the start and end of context and drops more than 30% when the answer sits in the middle. The information never leaves the window. Where it sits still changes whether the model uses it.

Tidy, evenly-structured input can degrade attention more than messy input. Uniform formatting makes it harder for a model to tell what's load-bearing.
Lost In The Middle · The Needle-In-A-Haystack Test

Look At This
Haystack!

Sixteen things parked in this lot. Fourteen are filler from a long back-and-forth about the same reserve report. One is the actual instruction the user gave. This is a hands-on version of the needle-in-a-haystack test — the standard way AI researchers check whether a model can still find one planted fact buried in a pile of irrelevant context. Try to spot it before reading the caption.

reserve
Q3
variance
ledger
reconciled
accrual
use 12%, not 8%
workpaper
closing
balance
adjustment
forecast
true-up
schedule
audit
memo
The instruction sits in row 2, between "accrual" and "workpaper." Nothing marks it as different. To the model, it's one token cluster among sixteen that all sound like they belong to the same conversation about reserves.
Live · You vs Claude

What Rate Did
They Actually Land On?

A real reserve thread — the discount rate changes four times, with a retraction and a final call. You read the whole thread. Claude gets it pushed to its terminal with the last two replies cut off — exactly what happens when a teammate pastes "the context" and misses the newest messages.

Your question: what single discount rate does Dana want used for the whole book? Put a number on your sticky note before you press the button.
The answer is 11% — Dana's final "lock it." Claude's copy stops just before the retraction, so it confidently answers the stale 10 / 12 split. You had the newest messages; it didn't. Whoever has the better context wins.
Working Around Context Rot

Six Ways To
Fight Context Rot

None of these fix the model — they change what it sees. Six patterns Claude composes on its own, each keeping one agent’s window small and clean.

01 · Route First

Triage & Route

A triage agent reads the input and hands it to the one specialist that fits. Nobody else sees it.

02 · Parallel Isolation

Fan-Out & Merge

Many agents work the same input in isolation; a synthesizer merges. Noise in one can’t touch another.

03 · Fresh Eyes

Adversarial Check

Each worker pairs with a skeptic that grades the output cold — no memory of how it was made.

04 · Many In, One Out

Generate & Filter

Flood drafts, keep the one that passes the rubric. The rest never reach the next step.

05 · Eliminate To One

Tournament

Candidates face off two at a time. Each judge sees only its pair — never the whole field.

06 · Spawn Fresh

Loop Until Done

Each step spawns a clean agent seeded by a short handoff note. The old transcript doesn’t travel.

The one they can’t automate is yours: put the critical instruction at the edges — the start or end, never buried in the middle.
The Pivot

Token
Management

If every token counts and the window is finite, the real skill is simple: put the right things in front of the model, in a form it can use.

Some levers move the needle far more than others. They're worth ranking.

Token Management · The Pecking Order

What Saves The
Most Tokens

Four levers, ranked by how much they move the needle. Get the top one right and the rest is fine-tuning.

  1. 1

    Document authorship & type biggest lever

    How you write and format what you feed it. A clean markdown doc costs a fraction of the same content as HTML or a raw export.

  2. 2

    The effort / reasoning setting big lever

    How hard the model thinks before answering. Max effort can burn 10× the tokens of low for the same prompt — most tasks don't need it.

  3. 3

    The model itself medium lever

    A small model for a small job. Routing simple tasks to a Haiku/Mini/Flash instead of a frontier model is often the easy win people skip.

  4. 4

    MCPs & tool calls silent leak

    Connectors are great — until a vague request makes the model hunt through call after call to find what it needs. This one balloons quietly.

Documentation Formats · Markdown

Markdown

The spine of agent docs. It's plain text, so it reads fine raw, but those same characters render into real structure. Left is what you type, right is what the model gets.

▸ What you type (raw)
---
title: Reconciliation
owner: finance-ops
---

# Reconciliation
## Monthly Steps

Run the **close** every *month*.

| Account | Variance | Status  |
|---------|----------|---------|
| AR-58   | $5,000   | open    |
| AP-12   | $120     | cleared |

1. Pull the `AR ledger`
   - filter to current quarter
   - exclude pending rows
2. Flag variance > $100 — see [runbook](https://wiki/recon)

- [x] Bank statement pulled
- [ ] Variances reviewed

```sql
SELECT account, variance
FROM ledger
WHERE variance > 250
ORDER BY variance DESC;
```
▸ What the model sees (rendered)

Reconciliation

Monthly Steps

Run the close every month.

AccountVarianceStatus
AR-58$5,000open
AP-12$120cleared
  1. Pull the AR ledger
    • filter to current quarter
    • exclude pending rows
  2. Flag variance > $100 — see runbook

☑Bank statement pulled
☐Variances reviewed

SELECT account, variance
FROM ledger
WHERE variance > 250
ORDER BY variance DESC;
Frontmatter (the --- YAML block) + headings (#=H1 … ######=H6) give the model an index to jump to the right section. A Claude SKILL.md like humanizer.md is exactly this — frontmatter on top, markdown body below.
Fun FactMarkdown was created in 2004 by John Gruber, with help from Aaron Swartz. The goal was radical: a format so readable that the raw text looks fine even if it's never rendered — which is exactly why LLMs love it.
Documentation Formats · XML

XML

It nests anything JSON can, to the same depth — except it wraps every value in both an opening and a closing tag. Machines find that bulletproof. Humans find it almost unreadable.

▸ Same data model as the JSON slide
<reconciliation>
  <account>AR-5847</account>
  <owner>finance-ops</owner>
  <variance>
    <amount>5000</amount>
    <currency>USD</currency>
    <status>open</status>
  </variance>
  <lines>
    <line doc="bank_stmt_apr" amt="3200"/>
    <line doc="ledger_q2" amt="1800"/>
  </lines>
</reconciliation>
▸ Why it makes humans cry
JSON:  "status": "open"
XML:   <status>open</status>
        ↑ the word "status" 3×

Every field is named twice — open tag and close tag. A deep model becomes a wall of </angle-brackets>. The machine doesn't care; your eyes do.

Great for: rigid schemas, config that must validate, legacy/enterprise systems (SOAP, .docx, RSS).
Weak for: humans, token budgets — all those repeated tags cost real money to feed a model.
Fun FactXML became a W3C standard in 1998, years before JSON. It powers things you use daily — every .docx, .xlsx, and RSS feed is XML underneath. JSON mostly won the web precisely because it dropped the closing tags.
Documentation Formats · JSON

JSON

YAML's strict cousin, with braces and quotes instead of whitespace. The same bytes can take two shapes: a machine happily reads the condensed one-liner, while a human needs the expanded tree. Either way it'll hold a whole nested data model.

▸ Condensed (what ships on the wire)
{"account":"AR-5847","owner":"finance-ops","variance":{"amount":5000,"currency":"USD","status":"open"},"lines":[{"doc":"bank_stmt_apr","amt":3200},{"doc":"ledger_q2","amt":1800}],"escalation":{"threshold":250,"route":["AP","controller"]}}

One unbroken string. Compact, exact, but unreadable to a human.

▸ Expanded (the same data, as a model)
{
  "account": "AR-5847",
  "owner": "finance-ops",
  "variance": {            // nested object
    "amount": 5000,
    "currency": "USD",
    "status": "open"
  },
  "lines": [               // array of objects
    { "doc": "bank_stmt_apr", "amt": 3200 },
    { "doc": "ledger_q2",   "amt": 1800 }
  ],
  "escalation": {          // object + array
    "threshold": 250,
    "route": ["AP", "controller"]
  }
}
Great for: nested data models, API payloads, config, glossaries — anything where structure must be exact and machine-parseable.
Weak for: humans skimming; punctuation-heavy, and every brace must match.
Fun FactJSON was never designed as a format — Douglas Crockford just discovered and named it in 2001 from how JavaScript already wrote objects. He famously said he "doesn't claim to have invented it, because it already existed in nature."
Documentation Formats · YAML

YAML

Think of it as lazy, nested markdown — indentation instead of brackets. Whitespace is the structure.

reconciliation:           # a key
  threshold_usd: 250      # nested value
  steps:                  # a list
    - pull_ledger
    - compare_bank_statement
  escalation:
    if: variance_over_threshold
    then: route_to_AP_team

Indent to nest. No closing tags, no braces. That's the whole trick.

Microsoft runs on YAML

  • Copilot Studio — topics & agent flows export as YAML.
  • Power Platform — Power Apps code components & .pa.yaml source files.
  • Azure Pipelines — azure-pipelines.yml defines every build.
  • GitHub Actions — workflows are .github/workflows/*.yml.
  • Azure Resource / Bicep tooling & AKS manifests.
If you touch the Microsoft stack, you already write YAML — you just may not have called it that.
Documentation Formats · CSV

CSV

It cuts two ways. A small relational map is about the best token bargain you'll find. Feed it a giant data dump, though, and you're just burning money.

Token Killer

Asking an agent to parse a 50k-row CSV burns enormous tokens to re-derive what a database query should do. Don't make the model your spreadsheet engine.

Great Token Value 
acronym,means
AR,Accounts Receivable
AP,Accounts Payable
Day1,AT&T Day 1 Billing
CMM,Customer Mgmt Module

A compact logical mapping — acronyms, lookups, relationships — is dense, unambiguous, and cheap. This is exactly what CSV is for.

Documentation Formats · HTML

HTML

A descriptive markup with decades of optimization baked in. It lands somewhere between XML's rigor and Markdown's ease, and unlike either of them, it can actually do things rather than just describe them.

Live: cost-of-funds

Annual interest:
$10,000

Drag a slider — the number recalculates live. That's the point of HTML over a slide.

Why it's the sweet spot

  • Descriptive — tags carry meaning, like XML, but lighter.
  • Battle-tested — every browser optimizes it; it just works.
  • Interactive — sliders, inputs, visuals a human can poke at, then present or hand to a model.
The cost: verbose for huge documents and abstract concepts — all those tags add up in tokens. Great for a focused interactive scenario; wasteful for a 200-page manual.
HTML, Proven

This Whole Deck
Is One HTML File

No PowerPoint. You can email that file, host it, or hand it straight to a model.

That's the payoff of HTML over slides for anything interactive: it does the thing instead of showing a picture of the thing.
Documentation Formats · PDF

PDF — Bad
vs Good

A PDF is a picture of a layout, not structured data. Sometimes that's a dealbreaker, sometimes it doesn't matter at all — and the difference comes down to where the data started life.

✗ Bad: a financial data table as PDF

Acct   Apr    May    Jun
AR-58  5,000  800    3,000
AP-12  —      3,000  120

Extracted, columns collide and rows scramble: AR-58 5,000 800 3,000 AP-12 3,000 120. The agent can't tell which number is which month. The structure is lost. Hand it the CSV instead.

✓ Good: a signed policy memo as PDF

RECONCILIATION POLICY v3
Effective 2026-01-01

All variances over $100 must be
reviewed within 5 business days...

Flowing narrative text, read top-to-bottom. The agent gets the full meaning cleanly — and you keep the signed, authoritative original. This is what PDF is for.

Rule of thumb: if the data was structured before it became a PDF, give the model the structured version. If it's narrative or a final record, the PDF is fine.
Fun FactPDF turned 32 in 2025 — Adobe created it in 1993 to make a page look identical on any printer. That print-first design is exactly why it's so awkward for AI: a PDF stores where text sits on a page, not what the text means.
Documentation Formats · The Cheat Sheet

Which Format,
When

Nine formats, one rule each. ✓ reach for it here — ✗ not here.

FormatUse it when…Avoid it when…
Markdown ✓Long-form docs, runbooks, skills — headings give the model an index. ✗You need strict, machine-validated structure.
YAML ✓Config and settings a human edits — clean, nested, readable. ✗Data has tricky strings — whitespace and colons bite.
JSON ✓Nested data models and API payloads that must parse exactly. ✗A human needs to read it comfortably at a glance.
XML ✓Rigid schemas, legacy/enterprise systems that demand it. ✗Tokens or human eyes matter — tags are repeated twice.
CSV ✓Small, flat lookup tables and mappings — a token bargain. ✗Data is nested, or it's a giant dump you're making the model parse.
PDF ✓Final records and narrative meant to be read as-is. ✗The data was structured first — give that version instead.
Word † ✓Narrative docs — once a tool extracts the text to markdown. ✗Handed over raw — layout and styles confuse the model.
Excel † ✓Tabular data — once a tool reads sheets into rows (or CSV). ✗Raw with formulas, merged cells, and tabs — it's a mess.
PowerPoint † ✓Slide text and notes — once a tool pulls them out. ✗As the binary file — shapes and positions aren't meaning.

† Office files (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx) can be read, but they don't translate well on their own — they're zipped bundles of XML, styles, and layout. A model needs a tool or plugin (Document Intelligence, a parser, Claude for Excel/Word/PowerPoint) to extract the actual content first.

The throughline: match the format to the job. If it has to be exact and machine-readable, reach for JSON or XML. If a person needs to read it, Markdown or YAML. CSV is for flat lookups, PDF for a final record, and Office files have to be extracted before they're any use. Pick wrong and you pay in tokens and accuracy.
Lever 2 · The Effort Setting

How Hard It Thinks

Effort is a dial — low, medium, high, max — for how much reasoning the model spends before it answers. It doesn't just affect the thinking; it governs every token in the response, including the text and the tool calls.

Prompt Generate an SVG of a pelican riding a bicycle.

Same prompt, five effort levels. Low barely holds together; max draws a proper pelican on a proper bike. You can see the extra reasoning — and pay for it.

The trap: the default leans high. On the same prompt, max can run roughly 10× the tokens of low, so it's worth matching effort to what's actually at stake rather than leaving it cranked all the way up. (Source: Claude effort & adaptive-thinking docs, 2026.)
Lever 3 · The Model Itself

The Window Doesn't
Travel With You

Switch to a smaller model mid-conversation and you can overflow it — older turns get dropped or compacted without warning. Switch to a bigger window and you've got more room, but you're paying for every token you put in it. Bigger isn't automatically better — models still do their best work when the window isn't stuffed to the brim.

The model also changes what the job costs. Small models run a fraction of the price on routine tasks — classifying, extracting fields, reformatting — and you usually can't tell the difference in output quality.

*200K standard, 1M in beta. Context sizes per vendor docs, 2026 — they change often.

TierModelContext
SMALL
fast & cheap		Claude Haiku 4.5200K
GPT-5 Mini400K
Gemini Flash1M
MEDIUM
the workhorse		Claude Sonnet 4.6200K*
GPT-5.4 Mini400K
Gemini 3 Flash1M
HIGH
save for hard work		Claude Opus 4.8200K*
GPT-5.41M
Gemini 3 Pro2M
EXTREME
why not		Claude Fable 51M

Fable 5: $50/M output tokens. Probably for Elon Musk.

Lever 4 · MCPs & Tool Calls

The Silent
Token Leak

An MCP wires the model up to a real system — your database, a BI tool, whatever you connect. That's hugely useful. The catch is that every tool call dumps its results back into the context window, so a vague request can send the model on a scavenger hunt of calls before it ever does the thing you asked for.

The same task — pulling a Power BI dataset — can cost wildly different amounts depending on how the request is phrased. The token count is the tell.

Power BI MCP · The Expensive Way

Vague Prompt → Scavenger Hunt

Prompt"Get data from the Power BI dataset PM_Reconciliation_data."

Token cost: ~48,000 (est. — every workspace & dataset list lands in context)

It doesn't know where that dataset lives, so it discovers the whole tree — one call at a time:

Four workspaces, four datasets each — 16 dataset names pulled into context just to locate one. Only the last call does real work; everything before it is overhead you paid for.

💡 Tip: you can just ask the model how it would use an MCP before letting it loose — e.g. "Without running anything, list the exact MCP tool calls you'd make to get the PM_Reconciliation_data dataset." It'll show you the scavenger hunt, and you'll know what to shortcut.
⚠ Warning: putting the model in auto / full-auto mode for complex MCP work is the fastest way to burn money — it'll happily make every discovery call without asking. Keep a hand on the wheel.
Power BI MCP · The Cheap Way

Specific Prompt → Straight There

Token cost: ~3,000 (est. — one lookup, one fetch)

Prompt: "Get data from the PM_Reconciliation_data dataset in the Finance Reconciliation workspace."

It still lists the workspaces once, but because you named the one you want, it calls get_datasets a single time instead of four — and skips the scan entirely:

get_workspacesstill has to list them
→
get_datasetsFinance Reconciliation only
→
get_dataPM_Reconciliation_data

Three calls instead of six. One workspace list, one dataset lookup, no fan-out and no scanning. Same answer, roughly 16× cheaper.

The model can only skip the scavenger hunt if it's given the landmarks up front. Specific prompts stay cheap.
Part Two

Organizing
Context

Managing tokens keeps the window lean. The other half of the job is arranging what's in it — so the model can find the right thing fast.

Layers, pointers, and a tidy house: how the pieces fit so nothing useful gets buried.

The Answer · How They Nest

Each Layer
Contains The Last

Here's how they fit together. They don't sit side by side — each one wraps the next. The harness holds the context, and the context holds the prompt.

Each layer contains the previous. Failure moves deeper with each evolution. 2022 → 2026+
Making It Real · The Global File

CLAUDE.md —
Your Global Rules

Those layers aren't theory. They're files. The top one is a single markdown file Claude reads at the start of every session, on every project.

Where it lives

# your home directory — applies everywhere
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md

How to create it

# in Claude Code, the # shortcut adds a memory
> #always explain code step by step

# or run /memory to open & edit the file
> /memory

What goes here

  • Things true across all your work — your preferences, how you like explanations, your default stack.
  • Loaded into the system prompt before you type anything.
  • This is your slow weights, on disk — written once, applied everywhere.
"Always write fully typed code. I prefer Tailwind. Explain step by step." — set it once, never repeat it again.
Making It Real · The Project File

The Project
CLAUDE.md

Drop a second CLAUDE.md inside a project folder. Claude walks up the tree and reads every one it finds — the more specific file wins when they disagree.

# global — you, everywhere
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md

# project — this repo, your team
~/projects/finance-agent/CLAUDE.md
                         # ↑ wins on conflict

Global = who you are. Project = what this codebase needs.

What goes in the project file

  • Build & test commands, naming conventions, architecture notes.
  • Team standards everyone sharing the repo should follow.
  • The stuff you'd otherwise re-explain at the start of every session.
Same idea as the nested layers: the project file sits inside the global one — closer to the work, so it carries more weight.
Making It Real · The Command

The /memory
Command

You've seen the files. /memory is the cockpit that drives them — it shows everything Claude currently has loaded and lets you edit it without hunting for files.

> /memory

# pick what to open & edit:
  1. Managed Policy   # org-wide
  2. Project Memory   # ./CLAUDE.md
  3. User Memory       # ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
  4. Local Project     # CLAUDE.local.md

Or skip the menu: press #, type a memory, hit Enter — it's filed into the right CLAUDE.md automatically.

What it's for

  • Audit what Claude actually knows right now — every loaded file and rule in one view.
  • Prune stale notes and add new ones without opening an editor.
  • Debug — if Claude ignores a rule, run /memory first; it may live in a file that hasn't loaded yet.
Auto-memory: Claude also writes its own notes as it works — build commands, conventions it figures out — so the file gets smarter without you typing.
The move that matters: deliberately promote what's worth keeping from your sticky notes (fast) into these files (slow). fast ➜ slow is how context survives past the moment it was useful.
Same Idea · Other Tools

It's Not Just
Claude

Every serious coding agent has this layered-memory idea. The filenames differ, and one of them is missing a layer entirely.

Claude CodeOpenAI CodexGitHub Copilot
Global file
(applies everywhere)		 Yes
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md		 Yes
~/.codex/AGENTS.md		 No first-class global
per-repo by design
Project file ./CLAUDE.md ./AGENTS.md .github/copilot-instructions.md
Per-path rules nested CLAUDE.md nested AGENTS.md .github/instructions/*.instructions.md (YAML frontmatter)
How they layer All three: files concatenate from broad → specific, and the more specific file wins on conflict. Copilot just skips the "you, everywhere" layer — your standards travel with the repo, not with you.

* Copilot has narrow exceptions — the CLI reads ~/.copilot/copilot-instructions.md and VS Code keeps user-profile instruction files — but neither is a true portable, machine-wide global, and they don't travel with the repo.

Scaling Up · Many Files

Organizing A
Knowledge Library

One file doesn't scale. The fix is the same trick a model uses: a small index that points to the right document, so nothing loads until it's needed.

knowledge/
├── index.md        # the map / table of contents
├── billing/
│   ├── day1-billing.md
│   └── invoiced-claims.md
├── reconciliation.md
└── glossary.md

# index.md — just pointers
- [Day 1 Billing](billing/day1-billing.md)
- [Reconciliation rules](reconciliation.md)
- [Acronyms](glossary.md)

Why an index of pointers

  • Claude reads the small index.md first, then follows the one pointer it needs — not the whole library.
  • Small, focused files = clean chunks that parse together correctly.
  • Link what's reference; inline only what's essential right here.
It's the headings-as-index idea again, just scaled up from one file to many. This is what lets a well-organized house hand Claude the right doc fast.
Organizing Context · Open Knowledge Format

A Standard For
The Index Pattern

Everything we just did — small files, an index of pointers, links between docs — Google Cloud turned into an open spec in June 2026: the Open Knowledge Format (OKF). It's a way to hand an agent curated knowledge that both people and models can read.

What it actually is

  • A directory of markdown files, each with a little YAML frontmatter block on top. That's the whole format.
  • Each file is one concept — a table, a metric, a runbook, a dataset. The file's path is its identity.
  • Files point at each other with ordinary markdown links, and those links form a knowledge graph.
  • No SDK, no database, no runtime. It renders on GitHub, ships as a tarball, and lives in version control next to your code.
Why it matters: most of what a model needs is internal knowledge — schemas, metric definitions, join paths — and today it's scattered across catalogs, wikis, and a few people's heads. OKF puts it in one portable place an agent can read and update directly.
Not RAG: a vector index re-derives knowledge from raw chunks every query. OKF stores curated, cross-linked concepts the agent reads straight from the files — closer to a wiki than a search index.
Open Knowledge Format · How It Works

Frontmatter
+ An Index

Two moving parts. Frontmatter tags each file so an agent knows what it's looking at, and an index.md at each level points the way in — so the model reads a map first and only opens the one file it needs.

▸ A concept file: frontmatter, then body
---
type: BigQuery Table          # the one required field
title: Orders
description: One row per completed order.
resource: bigquery://acme/sales/orders
tags: [sales, revenue]
timestamp: 2026-05-28T14:30:00Z
---

# Schema
| Column      | Type   | Notes              |
|-------------|--------|--------------------|
| order_id    | STRING | Unique order id.   |
| customer_id | STRING | FK to [customers](/tables/customers.md). |

The rules, in brief

  • Exactly one required field on every concept: type. The rest is up to you.
  • Reserved optional fields: title, description, resource, tags, timestamp.
  • That [customers](/tables/customers.md) link is the graph — one concept referencing another.
  • An index.md at each level gives progressive disclosure: the agent reads the map, then follows one pointer. A log.md can track change history.
This is the headings-as-index idea from a few slides ago — now with a tag on every file and a name everyone agrees on.
Open Knowledge Format · The Entry Point

index.md Checks
IDs At The Door

Every model wants in, but index.md only lets one pointer through at a time. That's the whole trick behind progressive disclosure — the map decides who gets past the rope, the rest of the bundle stays roped off until something actually asks for it.

Open Knowledge Format · A Worked Bundle

One Bundle,
End To End

Here's a small sales bundle. Ask an agent "how do I compute weekly active users?" and watch how it travels: read the top index, follow one link down, land on the concept that answers it.

▸ The bundle on disk
sales/
├── index.md            # the map
├── datasets/
│   ├── index.md
│   └── orders_db.md
├── tables/
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── orders.md
│   └── customers.md
└── metrics/
    ├── index.md
    └── weekly_active_users.md

How the agent walks it

  1. Reads sales/index.md — a short list of pointers, not the whole library.
  2. The question is about a metric, so it follows the one link to metrics/weekly_active_users.md.
  3. That concept's frontmatter says type: Metric; its body defines the calculation and links to tables/orders.md for the source.
  4. It opens orders.md, reads the schema, and assembles the answer — having loaded three small files instead of the entire catalog.
The result is curated and version-controlled, and it commits beside the SQL it describes.
Organizing Context · A Working Variation

The Project
Context Map

OKF is built to ship knowledge — portable bundles that travel between orgs. Here's a variation tuned for the other job: one person working across many live projects. Same index-and-pointer idea, pointed at a different target. I call it a Project Context Map (PCM).

The shape

  • A thin CLAUDE.md in each project points back to one centralized folder on your machine, organized by project.
  • That folder's index.md is the map: each entry is a file reference plus a one-line description of what it's for — written in frontmatter so the agent can decide whether to open it.
  • The agent reads the index, sees the descriptions, and chooses what to explore — pulling deep files only when the task needs them.
It's the same progressive-disclosure pattern as OKF — and the same router that Claude Code Skills use, where the agent reads descriptions first and loads the body only on a match.
Honest framing: this isn't a published spec, and it isn't technically better than OKF. It's the same pattern tuned for a different job — live, solo, multi-project work instead of a portable knowledge bundle.
Reference, don't inline: the project file points at deep storage instead of copying it, so the always-loaded layer stays small and the full store is one hop away.
Project Context Map · Same Bundle, Reorganized

The Sales Mock,
The PCM Way

Take Google's sales bundle. OKF organizes it by concept (tables, metrics). PCM organizes by project, and the index leads with a description so the agent routes itself.

▸ project file: a thin pointer
# ~/projects/sales-pipeline/CLAUDE.md
# (loads automatically when I cd here)

Context store for this project lives at:
  ~/context/sales/

Read ~/context/sales/index.md first.
Open only the entries you need.
▸ the index: descriptions as a router
---
project: Sales Pipeline
entries:
  - file: tables/orders.md
    desc: Order schema — one row per order.
  - file: metrics/weekly_active_users.md
    desc: WAU definition + how to compute it.
  - file: decisions/2026-q2.md
    desc: Why we switched the WAU window to 7d.
---

What changed from OKF

  • Organized by project, not concept. OKF groups tables/, metrics/, datasets/. PCM groups everything one project needs — including the things OKF leaves out.
  • The index carries the descriptions. Instead of opening each file's frontmatter to learn what it is, the agent reads one map and routes from the desc: lines.
  • Room for project memory. That decisions/2026-q2.md entry — the why behind a choice — has no natural home in a concept-only knowledge bundle. In PCM it sits right beside the schema.
  • It loads itself. CLAUDE.md inheritance pulls the pointer in the moment you open the project.
Project Context Map · When To Reach For It

Bundles Travel.
Projects Live.

Same pattern, different center of gravity. The question isn't which is better — it's whether you're shipping curated knowledge or working a live project.

OKF — Knowledge BundlePCM — Project Context Map
Optimized forPortable, vendor-neutral knowledge that travels between orgs and tools.One person moving across many live projects on their own machine.
Organized byConcept — tables, metrics, datasets, runbooks.Project — everything one effort needs, in one place.
HoldsCurated facts: schemas, definitions, join paths.Facts plus live state: decisions, status, the why.
Loads byExport / share the bundle; a tool reads it.CLAUDE.md inheritance — pulls itself in when you open the project.
Best whenThe knowledge outlives the project and needs to be shared.The context is yours, changes often, and spans many projects.
A codebase or data catalog fits OKF: the knowledge is durable and worth shipping. Day-to-day project work fits PCM: the context is live and personal, and easier to navigate grouped by the thing you're doing. Both are the index pattern. The difference is what the index points at.
Organizing Context · A Third Pattern

The Junkyard

OKF curates a knowledge bundle. PCM curates a project pointer. The Junkyard skips curation. Everything goes into one place — Notion, Obsidian, a folder of notes — and a search step finds what's relevant when the agent needs it.

The shape

  • One vault or workspace holds everything: meeting notes, specs, half-finished ideas, old decisions. Nothing gets filtered out before it goes in.
  • No required frontmatter, no index file, no folder discipline. Obsidian and Notion both work this way by default — write a note, save it, move on.
  • A retrieval layer does the sorting later. In Obsidian that's typically a plugin like Smart Connections; in Notion it's Notion AI or a connected search.
Curation has a cost. Writing frontmatter, naming concepts, and keeping an index current takes ongoing effort. Dropping everything into one app and trusting search to sort it out removes that cost entirely.
Same callback as the parking lot, at the scale of an entire vault: nothing gets evicted, nothing gets organized on the way in. Whatever finds the answer has to happen at read time.
The Junkyard · How It Finds Anything

Chunk, Embed,
Search

The mechanism behind both Notion AI and Obsidian's Smart Connections is the same: Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Before the model answers, a search step pulls the most relevant pieces of the vault into context.

The pipeline

  1. Every note gets split into chunks — a few hundred to a couple thousand characters each.
  2. Each chunk is converted into an embedding, a vector that represents its meaning.
  3. A question gets embedded the same way, and the search finds the chunks whose vectors are closest to it.
  4. The top few chunks get inserted into the model's context. The model answers using only those chunks.

Where it breaks

Chunk size is a real trade-off, not a setting to ignore. Chunks under a few hundred characters lose context — a sentence about a number with no record of what the number was for. Chunks over a few thousand characters drown the relevant line in everything else on the page, the same crowding problem from the context rot slides.

A note never gets reorganized by this process. It still lives wherever it was dropped. Only the chunk boundaries and the search ranking determine what the model sees.
The Junkyard · Where It Fits

Search Finds It.
Curation Knows It.

OKF and PCM put the structure in at write time. The Junkyard puts it in at read time, through search. Neither is wrong — they fit different amounts of upkeep.

OKF / PCM — Curated IndexJunkyard — RAG Over Everything
Structure addedAt write time — frontmatter, index, links.At read time — chunking and embedding search.
UpkeepSomeone maintains the index as things change.None. Drop a note in and move on.
What the agent getsThe exact file the index points to.Whichever chunks scored closest to the question.
Fails whenThe index goes stale and stops matching reality.The real answer is split across chunks, or buried under near-duplicates.
Best whenThe knowledge is stable enough to be worth indexing.The vault is large, messy, and growing faster than anyone can curate.
Most real vaults end up as a mix: a few load-bearing notes get an index and consistent tags, and everything else sits in the Junkyard until search needs to find it.
Obsidian & Notion · Used On Purpose

Filing Cabinet,
Not a Dump Site

The Junkyard works because the agent does the sorting at read time. But that's a fallback, not a goal. Used well, Obsidian and Notion are retrieval tools — drawers you go open to pull something specific, not a chute everything falls into.

What that looks like

  • Every note earns a label before it's saved — a tag, a folder, a one-line frontmatter description of what it is and why it exists.
  • You open the vault with a question, not to browse. "What did we decide about the schema" pulls one drawer, not the whole cabinet.
  • Dead notes get archived or deleted on a cadence. A drawer you never reopen is clutter, not context.
The failure mode isn't using Obsidian or Notion — it's treating either one as a junk drawer and hoping search will save you later. Search over noise still returns noise.
A filing cabinet rewards the person who labeled the folder months ago. The Junkyard doesn't ask that of you — which is exactly why it costs more at read time.
The Showdown

Are You
Smarter Than Claude?

One question. You answer from your sticky notes. Claude answers from this deck's own HTML. Whoever took the better notes wins.

"What is frontmatter in a markdown file, what's it for — and what format is it written in?"

Your context

Your sticky note. If you ran the three questions and caught the detail, you've got it. If not… "I should've written that down."

Claude's context

The full text of this presentation. Hand Claude the deck and the question — watch it answer from what it can see.

Good documentation isn't busywork. It's the difference between answering and guessing.

Bonus · It Was Never Academic

Claude's Own
Skills Use It

That frontmatter question wasn't trivia. Claude Skills — the reusable instructions that taught Claude to build Word docs, fill PDFs, even the skills behind this session — each declare themselves with exactly that YAML frontmatter block.

---
name: reference-to-roblox
description: Convert a reference image
  into a Roblox-ready mesh. Use when the
  user wants an asset from concept art.
---

# Reference → Roblox
## Instructions
1. Generate multi-view silhouettes
2. Run the mesh through Blender …

Every SKILL.md needs two frontmatter fields: name and description.

Why frontmatter, of all things?

  • Claude reads the description first to decide whether a skill is even relevant — before loading the body.
  • That's the --- metadata block doing exactly its job: tell the model what this is before it reads the whole thing.
  • Same principle as a clean heading tree or a tagged document — structure up front saves tokens and sharpens retrieval.
The thing you almost didn't write down is the thing Claude's entire skill system is built on.
Just For Fun · The Finale

Guess The
Pelican

Since December 2024, Simon Willison has run the same prompt against every new model that ships, and tracked the results. It's become a real running benchmark, in its own absurd way.

"Generate an SVG of a pelican riding a bicycle."

The rules

  • 8 real pelicans, real models, real prices.
  • Guess the model that drew it.
  • Guess what it cost to generate.
  • Order's shuffled — no cheating by "early ones look worse."
Costs are per-image API spend at the time — same prompt, wildly different bills depending which model you ask.
Pelican 1 of 8

Which Model
Drew This?
